Plaintiff David Baker'S Motion To Dismiss For Failure To Timely Join An Indispensable Party
Off Calendar Mar-14-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Robert DeVries, of Law Offices of Robert DeVries, Tel. 415.546.5100 ext 2000, for plaintiffs David Baker;
Joshua Safran, of Rudder Law Group, Tel. 510.384.7627, for respondents David Baker;
Sung E. Shim, of Katzoff & Riggs LLP, Tel. 510.588.5174, for plaintiffs David Baker;
Andrew Schwartz, of Sommers & Schwartz, LLP, Tel. 415.955.0925, for defendant Nina Mackenzie.

Ruling: A R G U E D. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. Respondents David Baker, an Architectural Corporation, and David Baker's motion to dismiss all causes of action alleged in the first amended petition filed by petitioner Nina MacKenzie for failure to timely join Kevin Wilcock as a party is denied as to all causes of action. Ms. MacKenzie's request for sanctions per CCP 128.5 is also denied. Mr. Wilcock is neither a necessary party per CCP 389(a) nor an indispensable party per CCP 389(b). He is not an indispensable party because all relief sought by Ms. MacKenzie can be afforded to her by the Baker parties and Mr. Wilcock does not claim an interest in the partnership alleged by Ms. MacKenzie. Complete relief can be afforded to Ms. MacKenzie on all her claims because the only monetary relief she seeks is payment of a "buyout price" by and damages against the Baker parties for their own alleged wrongdoing. Any declaration issued in this case will not be binding on Mr. Wilcock. All parties agree that Mr. Wilcock is not claiming any partnership interest. Even if Mr. Wilcock were a necessary party, the CCP 387(b) factors mandate that he is not an indispensable party. Because he is not claiming an interest in the alleged partnership and there has been no showing that there is any risk that he would be liable for any partnership debts, Mr. Wilcock's absence from this case is not prejudicial to him or to the existing parties. Moreover, the Baker parties' failure to assert any claims against Mr. Wilcock for indemnity, contribution or otherwise precludes them from arguing any prejudice to them by Mr. Wilcock's absence. The judgment can be shaped by stating that it has no effect on Mr. Wilcock since he was not a party to the case. A judgment rendered in Mr. Wilcock's absence is an adequate remedy for all existing parties. Most significantly, if Ms. MacKenzie's claims were dismissed because of her nonjoinder of Mr. Wilcock, she will be deprived of an adequate remedy for her claims against the Baker parties. Because this motion had at least arguable merit as to some of Ms. MacKenzie's claims and was not filed solely to harass Ms. MacKenzie or solely to cause unreasonable delay, Ms. MacKenzie's request for sanctions is denied. Order signed. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Felicia Green; Court Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR No. 8189, email: ckaren@sftc.org; Dept. 302.

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings
Off Calendar Mar-14-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Robert DeVries, of Law Offices of Robert DeVries, Tel. 415.546.5100 ext 2000, for plaintiffs David Baker;
Joshua Safran, of Rudder Law Group, Tel. 510.384.7627, for respondents David Baker;
Sung E. Shim, of Katzoff & Riggs LLP, Tel. 510.588.5174, for plaintiffs David Baker;
Andrew Schwartz, of Sommers & Schwartz, LLP, Tel. 415.955.0925, for defendant Nina Mackenzie.

Ruling: A R G U E D. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. PLAINTIFF DAVID BAKER, DAVID BAKER Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings on all causes of action alleged in the first amended petition filed by petitioner Nina MacKenzie is denied as to all causes of action. Ms. MacKenzie's request for sanctions per CCP 128.5 is also denied. To prevail on a statute of limitations argument on a motion for judgment on the pleadings or a demurrer, the date(s) establishing the running of the statute of limitations must clearly appear in the challenged pleading. (Romano v. County of Los Angeles (2000) 85 Cal.App.4th 316, 324-325.) The Baker parties argue that the statute of limitations began to run at some point between March and July of 2012, but nowhere on the face of Ms. MacKenzie's original petition or her first amended petition does it clearly appear when the statute of limitations began to run during that time period. Moreover, a breach of contract cause of action does not accrue (and thus the statute of limitations does not begin to run) before the time of breach. (Romano v. Rockwell Int'l Inc. (1996) 14 Cal.4th 479, 488.) Per the first amended petition, the Baker parties did not repudiate the alleged partnership agreement until August 23, 2012, when their counsel sent a letter denying the existence of the alleged partnership. Nothing in the petition clearly or necessarily states that the alleged oral partnership agreement was repudiated or breached at an earlier date than August 23, 2012. Consequently, even if (which is very doubtful) all of the claims alleged in the first amended petition are governed by the two year limitations period for oral contract claims, neither the petition nor the first amended petition discloses on its face that any of those claims are time-barred. The authorities relied on by the Baker parties concerning the statute of limitations running on the reasonable suspicion of a plaintiff are inapposite since those authorities concern the application of the delayed discovery rule for tort claims, not the date when a breach of contract claim accrues. Because this motion had at least arguable merit as to some of Ms. MacKenzie's claims and was not filed solely to harass Ms. MacKenzie or solely to cause unreasonable delay, Ms. MacKenzie's request for sanctions is denied. Order signed. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Felicia Green; Court Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR No. 8189, email: ckaren@sftc.org; Dept. 302.

Motion To Quash, Modify Or Limit Subpoena And Request For Monetary Sanctions
Off Calendar Jan-18-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Sung E. Shim of the law offices of Katzoff & Riggs LLP (510.588.5174) appears as counsel for plaintiffs/moving/opposing parties

Attorney, Frank F. Sommers of the law offices of Sommers & Schwartz (415.955.0925) appears as counsel for defendant/opposing party

No other appearance is made in this matter

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter. The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

Plaintiffs David Baker, an Architectural Corporation, and David Baker's Motion to Quash, Modify or Limit Subpoena and Request for Monetary Sanctions is DENIED, however all documents received from the subpoena may only be used by defendant Nina MacKenzie for this case and must not be disclosed to anyone besides those who have a need to know the information in the documents for their work on this case. Both sides' requests for monetary sanctions are denied. The report of the Judge Pro Tem states that requests 3, 4 and 5 in the subpoena are no longer in dispute. The documents sought in requests 1 and 2 are directly relevant to the central issue in the case of whether a partnership existed and the information contained in those document has not been shown to be available from other sources or ways less violative of plaintiffs' privacy interests. The existence of a partnership is often shown by the manner in which the entity claimed to be a partnership handles the money it receives and spends. The documents sought in requests 1 and 2 will provide highly relevant information on this issue. While plaintiffs' arguments have been rejected, they did not lack substantial justification. Indeed, as shown by the divergence of views of the court and those stated in the thoughtful report prepared by the Judge Pro Tem, reasonable minds can differ on the proper outcome of this motion. Written order is signed and filed in open court.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Jose Rios Merida; Reporter: Sherry L. Sawyer, CSR No. 5976, email: ssawyer@sftc.org. (D302)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Notice Of Motion Of Baker Parties For Summary Judgment Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Aug-29-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. ROBERT DEVRIES, TEL. NO. 1-415-546-5100 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. FRANK F. SOMMERS OF SOMMERS & SCHWARTZ, TEL. NO. 1-415-955-0925 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. PLAINTIFF DAVID BAKER, an architectural corporation and David Baker's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT/Summary Adjudication - The Baker Parties' motion for summary judgment and alternative motion for summary adjudication is denied in its entirety. The Baker Parties fail to show as a matter of law that no partnership existed. The Baker Parties failed to satisfy their burden of production and, even if they did, Ms. MacKenzie presented sufficient evidence showing that there is a triable issue regarding the existence of a partnership. The Baker Parties' separate statement shows that Mr. Baker created "David Baker + Partners, LLP" and registered that entity with the Secretary of State (UMF 10) and the retirement plan referred to "partners" (UMF 12). "Whether a partnership or joint venture exists is primarily a factual question to be determined by the trier of fact from the evidence and inferences to be drawn therefrom." (Bank of California v. Connolly (1973) 36 Cal.App.3d 350, 364.) Ms. MacKenzie presents passages from Mr. Wilcock's deposition where he, decedent, and Mr. Baker acted like a partnership. They would reach a consensus regarding salaries (47:1-12; 49:3-7; 75:20-25; 76:7-12; 83:11-15), the three had quarterly financial meetings (92:6-10), and the three made joint decisions regarding managing cash flow and expenses (94:1-95:16). Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Reyes Hunter, CSR # 6576 =302/hk
